name: Ricabo to Alto de Ventana Trail — circular via the GR 207
name_original: Pista de Ricabo a Alto de Ventana — circular por el GR 207
region_intro: Jogging routes around San Emiliano are set within the San Emiliano Valley Nature Park, characterized by rugged mountain reliefs and deep valleys. The region features significant natural landmarks such as Peña Ubiña, standing at 2,417 meters, and the San Emiliano Canyon, thickly covered by beech and oak trees. Glacial erosion and Karstic relief contribute to unique geological formations, offering diverse terrains for running. Best jogging routes around San Emiliano The most popular jogging route is Ascent to the Peñas Ubinas — circular from Tuíza, a difficult 8.1 miles (13.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 46 minutes to complete, offering challenging terrain and views of Peña Ubiña. Another top favourite among local runners is Medieval Route of Alesga Castle Loop, a difficult 5.0 miles (8.1 km) path. This route provides a historical jogging experience through varied landscapes. Local runners also love the Cordel de Merinas Trail, a moderate 5.7 miles (9.1 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes. Jogging around San Emiliano is defined by rugged mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and lush forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging ascents. The routes in San Emiliano are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars. Nearly 200 runners have used komoot to explore San Emiliano's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running routes in San Emiliano?
A: The running routes in San Emiliano are characterized by a diverse and striking natural landscape, featuring rugged mountain reliefs, deep valleys, and lush forests. You'll encounter clear examples of glacial erosion and Karstic relief, creating unique geological formations. Many trails offer shaded paths through beech and oak trees, as well as alpine meadows.

Q: Are there many challenging running routes in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, San Emiliano is known for its challenging terrain. Out of approximately 15 running routes available, the vast majority are rated as difficult. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240500705/ascent-to-the-penas-ubinas-circular-from-tuiza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Peñas Ubinas — circular from Tuíza&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 8.1-mile trail with significant elevation gain, offering breathtaking views.

Q: Are there any moderate running trails available in the area?
A: While most routes are challenging, there are some moderate options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056008921/cordel-de-merinas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cordel de Merinas Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.7-mile path that leads through varied landscapes and can typically be completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes.

Q: What natural landmarks or scenic viewpoints can I see while jogging in San Emiliano?
A: San Emiliano offers numerous natural landmarks and scenic viewpoints. You can enjoy views of the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5585412" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Ubiña&lt;/a&gt;, explore the San Emiliano Canyon with its dense beech and oak forests, or discover the picturesque Somiedo lakes. The region also features unique glacial and Karstic formations, and alpine meadows with views of reservoirs like La Cantarilla.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240525709/medieval-route-of-alesga-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Route of Alesga Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a historical jogging experience through varied landscapes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240500705/ascent-to-the-penas-ubinas-circular-from-tuiza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Peñas Ubinas — circular from Tuíza&lt;/a&gt;, providing challenging terrain and stunning mountain vistas.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in San Emiliano?
A: The running routes in San Emiliano are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Nearly 200 runners have explored the area, often praising the diverse terrain, breathtaking views, and the challenging yet rewarding nature of the trails.

Q: Can I spot any wildlife during my runs in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, the diverse flora and fauna of San Emiliano offer opportunities for wildlife spotting. The abundant woods are a habitat for various birds of prey, chamois, roe deer, deer, wild boars, and otters, especially around the Babia Valley and its lakes.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by lakes or reservoirs?
A: Absolutely. The Babia valley is home to the picturesque Somiedo lakes, offering serene routes amidst natural beauty. You can also find routes like the moderate loop from San Emiliano to Casa Mieres, which provides views of the La Cantarilla Reservoir. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24567099" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Lago de la Cueva – View of Lago de la Cueva loop from Alto de la Farrapona&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in San Emiliano?
A: San Emiliano is an excellent destination for outdoor activities thanks to its diverse natural landscapes. While specific seasonal advice isn't provided, the region's mountain environment suggests that spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery for running. Summer can also be good, especially in shaded areas or at higher altitudes.

Q: Are there any historical routes for running in San Emiliano?
A: Yes, you can combine your run with a historical experience. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240525709/medieval-route-of-alesga-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Route of Alesga Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique jogging experience through varied landscapes with historical significance.

Q: What are some of the mountain passes I might encounter or see on a run?
A: The region around San Emiliano features several notable mountain passes. Depending on your route, you might encounter or see passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6333018" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cubilla Pass&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240516498/ricabo-to-alto-de-ventana-trail-circular-via-the-gr-207" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ricabo to Alto de Ventana Trail — circular via the GR 207&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that incorporates such features.

Q: How long do the running trails in San Emiliano typically take to complete?
A: The duration of running trails in San Emiliano varies significantly based on their length and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1240500705/ascent-to-the-penas-ubinas-circular-from-tuiza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Peñas Ubinas — circular from Tuíza&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 8.1-mile trail, typically takes around 3 hours 46 minutes. A moderate route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056008921/cordel-de-merinas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cordel de Merinas Trail&lt;/a&gt;, at 5.7 miles, can be completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes.
tips: Beautiful track that starts from the port of Ventana and which is also elevated and where we can see good views of the area that goes down towards the center of Ricabo as the kilometers go by.

Puerto de Ventana is a mountain pass located in the Cantabrian Mountains, between the Principality of Asturias and the province of León. It lies at an altitude of 1,587 meters and connects the municipality of San Emiliano in León with the Asturian municipality of Teverga.

From the pass, you can enjoy a breathtaking view of the San Emiliano Valley, known as Babia de Yuso.

waypoints.name: Track from Ricabo to Alto de Ventana
